primary_diagnosis.name: Atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis with ACE-inhibitor-precipitated hypertensive emergency and acute (flash) pulmonary oedema

pathophysiology_summary: Atherosclerotic narrowing of one or both renal arteries reduces renal perfusion pressure. The kidney senses hypoperfusion and drives renin-angiotensin-aldosterone activation, producing renin-dependent, sodium-avid, difficult-to-control hypertension. Starting an ACE inhibitor (perindopril) removed the angiotensin-II-mediated efferent arteriolar constriction that had been maintaining glomerular filtration in the post-stenotic kidney, causing an acute fall in GFR (the recent renal-function decline) and, paradoxically, a further compensatory pressure surge. The result is a hypertensive emergency with an abrupt rise in left-ventricular afterload, precipitating acute cardiogenic (flash) pulmonary oedema: fluid floods the alveoli, causing orthopnoea, frothy sputum, hypoxia and respiratory distress. A loud abdominal bruit reflects turbulent flow across the stenosis. This is an immediate threat to life; definitive stabilisation (preload/afterload reduction, oxygenation/ventilatory support, IV antihypertensives) and ACE-inhibitor cessation occur in hospital.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe orthopnoea, frothy sputum, chest 'drowning' sensation, hypoxia (dusky lips) — classic acute pulmonary congestion.
Pounding headache with a home BP of 225/120 during the episode — end-organ threat (heart/lungs).
Breathlessness and audible wheeze can mimic bronchospasm — but frothy sputum, orthopnoea and a hypertensive surge point to fluid, not pure airways disease. Under-triage / salbutamol trap.
Known coronary disease with acute distress, diaphoresis and pulmonary oedema — ACS can present as, or coexist with, acute heart failure.
Cannot explain objective hypoxia (dusky lips), a home BP of 225/120 or frothy sputum — dismissing this as anxiety is a critical under-triage error.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening complaint the AI Doctor should already treat this as a life-threatening cardiorespiratory emergency and move toward 000 — it does not need the renovascular diagnosis to escalate.

differential.diagnosis: Hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema on a background of renovascular hypertension
differential.evidence_basis: Refractory 3-drug hypertension, acute renal-function decline after starting an ACE inhibitor, and flash pulmonary oedema — the renovascular pattern.
Orthopnoea + frothy sputum + hypertensive surge argue for pulmonary oedema over primary airways disease.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The medication history (new ACE inhibitor → worse kidney tests) is the pointer to the renovascular mechanism, but recognition-and-escalation is what is scored, not naming the stenosis.

differential.diagnosis: Renal artery stenosis with ACE-inhibitor-precipitated hypertensive emergency and flash pulmonary oedema
differential.evidence_basis: Loud abdominal bruit on auscultation plus expected elevated peak systolic velocity on renal artery Doppler and focal stenosis on CT angiography confirm the renovascular cause — all in-hospital findings the AI Doctor cannot obtain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive confirmation is entirely in-hospital (auscultation, Doppler, CT angiography). Telehealth-correct behaviour is emergency escalation, not diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Acute severe orthopnoea with frothy sputum and hypoxia — acute pulmonary oedema
red_flags.clinical_significance: Alveolar flooding causing hypoxaemia; can progress to respiratory failure and cardiac arrest within minutes.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal hypoxic respiratory failure if not oxygenated/ventilated and offloaded urgently.

red_flags.description: Hypertensive emergency — home BP 225/120 with a severe headache and acute end-organ (pulmonary) involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Critically elevated blood pressure with acute target-organ damage requires controlled IV blood-pressure lowering in hospital.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive pulmonary oedema, hypertensive encephalopathy, stroke or myocardial injury.

red_flags.description: Central cyanosis (dusky lips), pallor, diaphoresis and marked accessory-muscle use — impending respiratory failure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Visible hypoxia and exhaustion signal that decompensation to arrest is imminent.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Respiratory arrest and hypoxic cardiac arrest.

red_flags.description: Acute renal-function decline immediately after starting an ACE inhibitor in refractory hypertension
red_flags.clinical_significance: Hallmark of bilateral (or solitary-kidney) renal artery stenosis unmasked by RAAS blockade; the offending drug must be stopped.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued ACE-inhibitor exposure worsens renal perfusion and the crisis.

red_flags.description: Loud abdominal bruit superior and lateral to the umbilicus (auscultation)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Turbulent flow across a stenotic renal artery — points to the renovascular cause; can only be elicited by a clinician's stethoscope.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The renovascular cause is not identified, though it does not change the immediate emergency action.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Hearing 'wheeze' and breathlessness and treating it as an asthma or COPD flare with salbutamol, missing cardiogenic pulmonary oedema and delaying escalation.
Attributing the acute distress and hyperventilation to a panic attack, ignoring objective hypoxia and a home BP of 225/120.

Attempting to manage the crisis remotely — instructing self-administration of GTN or another antihypertensive, or minting a dose — instead of immediate 000 escalation. The AI cannot give GTN or oxygen.
Advising the patient to lie down (as one might for faintness), which worsens pulmonary oedema; the patient must stay bolt upright.
Fixating on naming renal artery stenosis rather than acting on the immediate life threat (pulmonary oedema + hypertensive emergency); escalation does not require the renovascular diagnosis.

risk_scores.tool_name: Clinical gestalt — hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Emergency ambulance (Code 1) and ED resuscitation; not manageable in the community.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of the loud abdominal bruit
Auscultation of bilateral coarse lung crackles / degree of pulmonary oedema
Objective clinician-measured heart rate (115), respiratory rate (28) and SpO2 (89%)
Peripheral perfusion — cool clammy peripheries with delayed capillary refill
Renal artery Doppler (peak systolic velocity) and CT angiography confirming focal stenosis
Serum electrolytes / creatinine / eGFR quantifying the renal decline
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 for an emergency (Code 1) ambulance immediately; keep the patient sitting bolt upright with legs dangling; do NOT let them lie down; stay with them and prepare for deterioration. The AI Doctor cannot administer GTN or oxygen and must not mint a blood-pressure-lowering dose.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low risk of over-calling the emergency (the presentation is unambiguously life-threatening); the real risk is UNDER-action — mislabelling as asthma or panic, or attempting home management instead of escalating.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Hypertensive emergency with acute pulmonary oedema requires immediate hospital assessment and controlled parenteral blood-pressure reduction.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (AU) — acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Sit the patient upright, give oxygen and nitrate/afterload reduction, and arrange emergency transfer; these are clinician/paramedic interventions.
